definition: A range-bound phase where price oscillates between a defined high and low without directional expansion, marked by small-bodied, overlapping candles and indecision. It follows and precedes expansion, representing accumulation in AMD/Power of Three frameworks. Resolution typically occurs via a manipulation sweep of one extreme followed by a change in state of delivery.
identification_rules: Price oscillates sideways between a clearly defined high and low without directional displacement or expansion closes beyond the range.
Range boundaries are tested multiple times without a closing breakout; price fails to make new highs or lows beyond the extremes.
Candles inside the range are small-bodied, overlapping, often with long wicks (indecision candles) and can include inside bars.
Equal highs and/or equal lows frequently form at the boundaries, indicating liquidity pools at the extremes.
No protected swing forms; closures against the anticipated direction are slow (more than 1–3 candles) and lack a swift V-shaped recovery through opposing series.
The phase appears between expansion legs, typically following a large-range expansion candle or several consecutive directional candles.
On lower time frames, price action is choppy and structureless; the range becomes clearer when stepping up to a higher time frame.
A valid resolution requires a sweep (manipulation wick) of one extreme followed by a closure back inside and a lower-time-frame change in state of delivery (CISD) or aggressive displacement.
Consolidation may involve a sweep of one side without the other, forming an asymmetric structure that hints at the next breakout direction.
At key levels, long wicks or multiple touches without effective breakout are observed.
trading_implication: Do not trade within the consolidation; wait for a manipulation sweep of the range high or low. After the sweep, look for a change in state of delivery (CISD) or aggressive displacement back inside to confirm direction, then enter in the opposite direction, targeting the opposite extreme or external liquidity. In an uptrend, prefer longs after a sweep of the range low; in a downtrend, shorts after a sweep of the range high. The consolidation is an accumulation phase; its resolution is the next expansion.
common_mistakes: Trading inside the consolidation before one side of the range is swept.
Entering on a sweep without waiting for a change in state of delivery (CISD) or a closure back inside to confirm direction.
Mistaking consolidation for a retracement or reversal, leading to premature directional entries.
Trusting equal highs/lows as reversal signals when price merely touches them without a genuine sweep and CISD.
Marking and trading off internal order blocks or levels inside the range, which are unreliable.
Forcing a trend continuation after a large expansion without accounting for the subsequent consolidation phase.
Expecting a wick to be respected as a reversal inside consolidation rather than recognizing it as a liquidity sweep.
Treating slow closures or doji candles as continuation signals when they often indicate pending sweeps.
Pairing currencies of similar relative strength/weakness, expecting a trend, which results in consolidation.
Using wicks instead of candle bodies to define the range in box setups, leading to incorrect boundaries.
Confusing consolidation with retracement: retracement forms a protected swing, while consolidation does not necessarily.
Ignoring CSD confirmation and entering based solely on higher time frame candle closes.
Chasing continuation patterns within consolidation after higher time frame targets have been met.
Failing to confirm consolidation structure across multiple time frames.
Ignoring SMT relative strength signals, leading to wrong trade direction.

related_concepts.term: Change in the state of delivery

merge_notes: School conflict: 3 cards (ICT, ICT Updated, Price Action) vs 1 SMC; picked Price Action by majority. The 'Range' card from ICT Updated defines a different concept (premium/discount range) but was included as a related concept; its identification rules were not merged into consolidation rules. SMC card had only 3 rules, all subsumed by ICT rules.
